meta.name: Container (Basic)
meta.description: Linux namespace isolation, cgroups resource limits, and rootless container support
meta.essence: Kernel-level process and resource isolation using namespace syscalls (clone, unshare) for PID, mount, network, UTS, and user namespace separation, combined with cgroup controllers to partition CPU, memory, and process resources, and pivot_root for filesystem isolation — all without hardware virtualization.
meta.why_important: Building containers from scratch demystifies Docker/Kubernetes internals and teaches fundamental Linux kernel primitives used in production container orchestration, making you effective at debugging container issues, understanding security boundaries, and optimizing containerized deployments.
learning_outcomes: Implement process isolation using PID namespaces with clone() or unshare() syscalls
Configure mount namespaces with bind-mount + pivot_root to isolate filesystem views
Create network namespaces and virtual ethernet pairs for container networking
Apply cgroup controllers to enforce CPU, memory, and process resource limits
Implement user namespace mapping for rootless (unprivileged) container execution
Debug namespace-related issues by understanding /proc filesystem mechanics
Build a minimal container runtime handling process lifecycle and cleanup
Understand container escape vulnerabilities and mitigation strategies

milestones.name: PID and UTS Namespace Isolation
milestones.description: Isolate process tree with PID namespace and hostname with UTS namespace.
acceptance_criteria: Create new PID namespace using clone(CLONE_NEWPID) or unshare(CLONE_NEWPID); child process sees itself as PID 1
Parent process observes the child's real host PID via the return value of clone() or waitpid()
Container init (PID 1) properly reaps zombie child processes using waitpid(-1, ..., WNOHANG) in a loop
UTS namespace (CLONE_NEWUTS) isolates hostname; sethostname() inside container does not affect host
Verify isolation by reading /proc/self/status and comparing NSpid field from inside vs outside the namespace
pitfalls: clone() requires a properly allocated and aligned stack passed as the child's stack pointer; on x86-64 pass the TOP of the stack (stack grows downward)
PID 1 in a namespace has init responsibilities: it must reap orphaned children or they become zombies that can never be collected
unshare(CLONE_NEWPID) affects children, not the calling process itself; the caller must fork() after unshare for PID 1 behavior
Forgetting CLONE_NEWUTS means hostname changes inside the container leak to the host
deliverables: PID namespace creation using clone() or unshare() + fork()
UTS namespace creation with independent hostname
Process isolation verification comparing inner and outer PIDs
Init process (PID 1) zombie reaping loop

milestones.name: Mount Namespace and Filesystem Isolation
milestones.description: Isolate filesystem mounts using mount namespace with pivot_root for complete root filesystem replacement.
acceptance_criteria: New mount namespace (CLONE_NEWNS) isolates mount/unmount operations from the host
Mount propagation is set to private (mount --make-rprivate /) preventing mount events from leaking to host
New root directory is bind-mounted onto itself (mount --bind newroot newroot) making it a mount point suitable for pivot_root
pivot_root() atomically swaps the root filesystem; old root is mounted at a subdirectory inside the new root
Old root is unmounted (umount2 with MNT_DETACH) after pivot_root, making host filesystem completely inaccessible
Essential pseudo-filesystems (/proc, /sys, /dev) are mounted inside the container with appropriate flags (e.g., proc with nosuid,noexec)
Container processes cannot access any host filesystem path after pivot_root + old root unmount
pitfalls: pivot_root(2) requires new_root to be a mount point; without the bind-mount-to-self trick, it fails with EINVAL
Forgetting MS_REC | MS_PRIVATE on the root mount propagation allows mount events to leak bidirectionally
Not unmounting the old root after pivot_root leaves the entire host filesystem accessible from within the container
Mounting /proc without the PID namespace means the container sees host processes
Device nodes in /dev require special handling; bind-mount only the minimum set needed (null, zero, urandom)
deliverables: Mount namespace creation with private propagation
Bind-mount-to-self for new root preparation
pivot_root() call swapping container root filesystem
Old root unmount ensuring host filesystem inaccessibility
/proc, /sys, /dev mounting inside the container namespace
Minimal rootfs directory structure verification

milestones.name: Network Namespace and Container Networking
milestones.description: Isolate network stack and establish connectivity via veth pairs and a bridge.
acceptance_criteria: New network namespace (CLONE_NEWNET) provides isolated interfaces, routing table, and iptables rules
veth pair is created with one end in the container namespace and one end on the host
Host-side veth end is attached to a Linux bridge (e.g., ctr0); bridge has an IP address serving as the container gateway
Container-side veth has an IP address assigned and a default route pointing to the bridge IP
Loopback interface inside the container is brought up (ip link set lo up)
Container can reach external networks via NAT (iptables MASQUERADE on the host's outbound interface)
DNS resolution works inside the container via a bind-mounted or generated /etc/resolv.conf
pitfalls: Creating the veth pair before the network namespace exists; the pair must be created then one end moved with 'ip link set <veth> netns <pid>'
Forgetting to bring up the loopback interface inside the container; many applications depend on localhost
NAT/MASQUERADE rules require IP forwarding enabled on the host (sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward=1)
DNS resolution fails silently without /etc/resolv.conf inside the container; always configure it
Not cleaning up veth pairs and bridge on container exit, leaking network resources
deliverables: Network namespace creation for isolated network stack
veth pair setup with one end moved into container namespace
Linux bridge configuration connecting container veth endpoints
IP address and route configuration on both ends
NAT/MASQUERADE rules for outbound internet access
DNS configuration inside the container

milestones.name: Cgroups Resource Limits
milestones.description: Limit CPU, memory, and process count using cgroups v2 (with v1 fallback awareness).
acceptance_criteria: Detect whether the system uses cgroups v1 or v2 by checking /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.controllers existence
Create a cgroup for the container and write the container PID to cgroup.procs before exec
Set memory.max (v2) or memory.limit_in_bytes (v1) and verify OOM killer terminates the process when exceeded
Set cpu.max (v2) with quota and period (e.g., '50000 100000' for 50% CPU) and verify CPU throttling under load
Set pids.max to cap the number of processes; verify fork bomb is contained
Clean up the cgroup hierarchy (remove cgroup directory) on container exit to prevent resource leaks
Report resource usage (memory.current, cpu.stat) for observability
pitfalls: cgroups v2 uses a unified hierarchy with different file names than v1; code must handle the active version
Writing to cgroup files requires the container PID to already exist; write to cgroup.procs after clone/fork but before exec
memory.max does not account for kernel memory by default in v2; kernel memory is included automatically
Cleanup order matters: all processes must exit before the cgroup directory can be removed (rmdir)
Not enabling the memory and pids controllers in the parent cgroup's cgroup.subtree_control prevents child cgroups from using them
deliverables: cgroup version detection (v1 vs v2)
cgroup creation and process assignment
Memory limit configuration with OOM verification
CPU limit configuration with quota/period throttling
Process count limit preventing fork bombs
cgroup cleanup on container exit

milestones.name: User Namespace and Rootless Containers
milestones.description: Implement user namespace mapping to enable unprivileged (rootless) container execution.
acceptance_criteria: User namespace (CLONE_NEWUSER) is created allowing an unprivileged user to appear as root (UID 0) inside the container
UID/GID mapping is written to /proc/<pid>/uid_map and /proc/<pid>/gid_map before the container process calls exec
'deny' is written to /proc/<pid>/setgroups before writing gid_map (required by kernel for unprivileged user namespace creation)
Container process running as mapped UID 0 can perform mount operations and other privileged operations within its namespaces
Outside the container, the process is observed running as the original unprivileged UID
All previous milestones (PID, mount, network, cgroups) function correctly when combined with user namespace
pitfalls: uid_map and gid_map must be written exactly once by the parent process; writing from within the namespace or writing twice fails
setgroups must be set to 'deny' before writing gid_map for unprivileged users (kernel security requirement since Linux 3.19)
User namespace must be created first (or simultaneously with CLONE_NEWUSER | CLONE_NEWPID | ...) for other namespaces to work without root
Network namespace setup with veth pairs still requires host-side root privileges; rootless networking typically uses slirp4netns or pasta
Some cgroup operations require delegation setup (systemd cgroup delegation) for rootless cgroup control
deliverables: User namespace creation with CLONE_NEWUSER
UID/GID map writer configuring identity mapping (e.g., 0 1000 1)
setgroups deny configuration for unprivileged gid_map
Integration test running full container stack as unprivileged user
Documentation of limitations (networking, cgroup delegation)

blueprint.title: Container (Basic)
blueprint.overview: Build a minimal container runtime from scratch using Linux kernel primitives—namespaces, cgroups, and user namespace mapping—to achieve process isolation without hardware virtualization. This project strips away the abstraction layers of Docker and Kubernetes to reveal the actual syscalls and kernel mechanisms that make containers possible. You'll implement PID isolation, mount namespace filesystem isolation with pivot_root, network namespace virtualization with veth pairs, cgroup resource limits, and unprivileged rootless container execution through user namespace UID/GID mapping.
blueprint.design_philosophy: Containers are often treated as magical black boxes, but they're simply processes with kernel-enforced isolation boundaries. By implementing each namespace type and resource control mechanism directly via syscalls, learners understand exactly what the kernel provides and where the boundaries lie. This knowledge is essential for debugging container issues, understanding escape vulnerabilities, and making informed decisions about container security. The progression from PID namespace (simplest) through user namespace (most complex) mirrors how the kernel itself evolved these features.

implementation.rationale: Container primitives are Linux kernel syscalls (clone, unshare, mount, pivot_root) with specific flag values and memory layout requirements. C provides direct syscall access via glibc wrappers, explicit stack management for clone(), and precise control over process lifecycle. The project involves reading/writing /proc files, manipulating file descriptors, and managing process state—operations that are clearest in C without runtime abstractions.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case for functions and variables, UPPER_CASE for constants and flags. Explicit error checking after every syscall with perror() for debugging. Use static functions for internal helpers. Include <sys/types.h>, <sys/wait.h>, <sched.h>, <unistd.h>, <sys/mount.h>, <sys/syscall.h> as needed. Align stack allocations to 16-byte boundaries for clone().
implementation.build_system: Makefile with gcc, linking against -lc for glibc. Separate targets for each milestone binary plus integration tests.
alternatives: Go (runc implementation language, goroutine-safe, but hides syscall details)
Rust (memory-safe, good for production runtimes, but borrow checker adds complexity for learning)

assumed_known: C programming with pointers and structs
Process lifecycle (fork, exec, wait, exit codes)
Basic file I/O and file descriptors
Shell commands for process inspection (ps, top, /proc)

must_teach_first.concept: clone() syscall and child stack allocation
Network namespace device movement via netlink

milestones.title: PID and UTS Namespace Isolation
milestones.summary: Create your first container boundary by isolating the process tree (PID namespace) and hostname (UTS namespace). Learn why PID 1 has special responsibilities and how clone() differs from fork() for namespace creation.
milestones.misconception: Developers often believe containers are lightweight VMs with their own kernel. They assume namespace isolation is complex magic performed by container runtimes.
milestones.reveal: A container is just a process tree with kernel-enforced blinders. PID namespace is simply a view transformation—process 12345 on the host becomes process 1 inside the namespace. The kernel maintains both views simultaneously via NSpid fields. clone(CLONE_NEWPID) costs almost nothing—it's just setting a flag that changes how the kernel translates PIDs for that process hierarchy.
cascade: Process tree isolation — understand how Docker's 'init' process (PID 1 in container) differs from host init, and why signal handling differs
Zombie reaping — PID 1 ignores SIGCHLD by default, must explicitly wait() on children or they become unkillable zombies (applies to any long-running daemon, not just containers)
Hostname isolation (UTS) — same kernel struct (struct uts_namespace) pattern used for other namespaces, shows the generic namespace infrastructure
Cross-domain: Kubernetes pod sandbox — gVisor/Kata use similar PID namespace isolation but add extra boundaries; understanding raw namespaces helps debug 'why can't my pod see host processes'
Security boundary limits — namespaces isolate views, not resources; a container can still consume unlimited memory without cgroups (next milestone)
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Create new PID namespace using clone(CLONE_NEWPID) or unshare(CLONE_NEWPID); child process sees itself as PID 1
Parent process observes the child's real host PID via the return value of clone() or waitpid()
Container init (PID 1) properly reaps zombie child processes using waitpid(-1, ..., WNOHANG) in a loop
UTS namespace (CLONE_NEWUTS) isolates hostname; sethostname() inside container does not affect host
Verify isolation by reading /proc/self/status and comparing NSpid field from inside vs outside the namespace

milestones.title: Mount Namespace and Filesystem Isolation
milestones.summary: Achieve true filesystem isolation by combining mount namespaces with pivot_root—the atomic root filesystem swap that makes chroot look like a toy. Learn why bind-mount-to-self is necessary and how mount propagation can leak isolation.
milestones.misconception: Many developers think chroot provides container filesystem isolation, or that mount namespaces automatically prevent host filesystem access.
milestones.reveal: chroot only changes the process's view of '/' but doesn't prevent access to the old root via /proc/pid/root or open file descriptors. pivot_root atomically swaps the root filesystem mount point AND moves the old root to a subdirectory, which you then unmount. The bind-mount-to-self trick is required because pivot_root demands that new_root be a mount point—not just a directory. Mount propagation (MS_SHARED vs MS_PRIVATE) can silently leak mounts between namespaces if not explicitly set to private.
cascade: Mount propagation types — MS_SHARED, MS_PRIVATE, MS_SLAVE, MS_UNBINDABLE control how mount events propagate across namespaces; shared mounts are the default on modern Linux and leak container mounts to host

pivot_root vs chroot security — pivot_root changes the root mount point in kernel mount table; chroot only changes the process's current root directory pointer. Escaping chroot via mkdir + chroot is a classic vulnerability.
Container image layers — Docker's overlayfs is just a special mount type; understanding mount namespaces explains how union filesystems work
Cross-domain: Database storage engines — PostgreSQL's tablespaces, MySQL's innodb_file_per_table use similar mount isolation concepts for data directory management
Rootfs construction — why containers need /proc, /sys, /dev even in minimal images; these aren't directories but kernel interfaces that must be mounted
yaml_acceptance_criteria: New mount namespace (CLONE_NEWNS) isolates mount/unmount operations from the host
Mount propagation is set to private (mount --make-rprivate /) preventing mount events from leaking to host
New root directory is bind-mounted onto itself (mount --bind newroot newroot) making it a mount point suitable for pivot_root
pivot_root() atomically swaps the root filesystem; old root is mounted at a subdirectory inside the new root
Old root is unmounted (umount2 with MNT_DETACH) after pivot_root, making host filesystem completely inaccessible
Essential pseudo-filesystems (/proc, /sys, /dev) are mounted inside the container with appropriate flags (e.g., proc with nosuid,noexec)
Container processes cannot access any host filesystem path after pivot_root + old root unmount

milestones.title: Network Namespace and Container Networking
milestones.summary: Give your container its own network stack—separate interfaces, routing table, iptables rules. Connect it to the outside world via virtual ethernet pairs and a Linux bridge, then enable outbound internet access through NAT.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think container networking is provided by Docker's bridge network driver, or that containers inherit the host's network stack by default.
milestones.reveal: Network namespace gives the container a completely separate network stack: its own loopback, its own routing table, its own iptables rules, even its own socket namespace. A new network namespace starts with only lo (down) and no connectivity. veth pairs are virtual cables—you create both ends in the host's network namespace, then move one end into the container's namespace via setns() or by specifying the target netns at creation. The bridge is just a virtual switch; containers are like devices plugged into it.
cascade: veth pair mechanics — veth devices always come in pairs; traffic into one end emerges from the other. They're used in Docker, Kubernetes pod networking (CNI plugins), and even for connecting VMs.
Linux bridge as virtual switch — bridge learns MAC addresses, forwards frames, supports VLAN filtering. Same technology used by OpenStack Neutron, libvirt.
NAT and container networking — MASQUERADE rule rewrites container source IP to host IP for outbound traffic. This is why containers can reach internet but aren't directly reachable from outside.
Cross-domain: Kubernetes CNI plugins — Flannel, Calico, Cilium all use network namespaces + veth pairs + bridges (or similar). Understanding raw namespace networking helps debug CNI issues.
DNS in containers — /etc/resolv.conf must be configured inside the container; it's not inherited from namespace. Kubernetes injects cluster DNS via this file.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: New network namespace (CLONE_NEWNET) provides isolated interfaces, routing table, and iptables rules
veth pair is created with one end in the container namespace and one end on the host
Host-side veth end is attached to a Linux bridge (e.g., ctr0); bridge has an IP address serving as the container gateway
Container-side veth has an IP address assigned and a default route pointing to the bridge IP
Loopback interface inside the container is brought up (ip link set lo up)
Container can reach external networks via NAT (iptables MASQUERADE on the host's outbound interface)
DNS resolution works inside the container via a bind-mounted or generated /etc/resolv.conf

milestones.title: Cgroups Resource Limits
milestones.summary: Enforce hard resource boundaries—CPU throttling, memory limits with OOM killing, and process count caps. Learn the difference between cgroups v1 (legacy, multiple hierarchies) and v2 (unified, modern default) and why cleanup order matters.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think namespaces provide resource isolation, or that cgroups are just for monitoring container resource usage.
milestones.reveal: Namespaces only control visibility, not consumption. A process in a PID namespace can still use 100% of host CPU and all RAM. cgroups are the enforcement mechanism—they're a filesystem API (/sys/fs/cgroup) where you write limits and the kernel actively throttles or kills. cgroups v2 unified everything into a single hierarchy; v1 had separate hierarchies for each controller. OOM killer in cgroups is per-cgroup: when memory.max is exceeded, only that cgroup's processes get killed, not random host processes.
cascade: CPU throttling mechanics — cpu.max '50000 100000' means 50ms of CPU time per 100ms period. The kernel's CFS bandwidth controller enforces this via real-time throttling.
Memory limits and OOM — memory.max is the hard limit; exceeding it triggers cgroup-aware OOM killer. memory.high is a soft limit that triggers reclaim pressure.
Process count limits (pids.max) — prevents fork bombs from taking down the host. Same technique used by systemd's TasksMax.

Cross-domain: Kubernetes resource requests/limits — k8s resource.requests becomes cgroup memory.min/cpu.weight; resource.limits becomes memory.max/cpu.max. Understanding cgroups helps debug 'why is my pod being OOMKilled' issues.
cgroup delegation — for rootless containers, cgroup control requires delegation from parent cgroup (via systemd or cgroup.subtree_control)
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Detect whether the system uses cgroups v1 or v2 by checking /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.controllers existence
Create a cgroup for the container and write the container PID to cgroup.procs before exec
Set memory.max (v2) or memory.limit_in_bytes (v1) and verify OOM killer terminates the process when exceeded
Set cpu.max (v2) with quota and period (e.g., '50000 100000' for 50% CPU) and verify CPU throttling under load
Set pids.max to cap the number of processes; verify fork bomb is contained
Clean up the cgroup hierarchy (remove cgroup directory) on container exit to prevent resource leaks
Report resource usage (memory.current, cpu.stat) for observability

milestones.title: User Namespace and Rootless Containers
milestones.summary: Run containers without root privileges by mapping container UID 0 to an unprivileged host UID. Understand the subtle ordering requirements for uid_map/gid_map and why setgroups='deny' is mandatory for unprivileged user namespace creation.
milestones.misconception: Developers think running containers requires root on the host, or that user namespaces automatically map all UIDs correctly.
milestones.reveal: User namespace is the key to rootless containers. Inside the namespace, you appear as root (UID 0) and can do root-like things within your namespaces (mount filesystems, create device nodes). Outside, you're still UID 1000. The mapping is explicit: write '0 1000 1' to uid_map meaning 'container UID 0-0 maps to host UID 1000-1000'. The kernel enforces strict ordering: parent must write uid_map/gid_map before child execs, and setgroups must be 'deny' before writing gid_map for unprivileged users (security fix for CVE-2014-8989).
cascade: UID/GID mapping mechanics — multiple ranges allowed: '0 100000 65536' maps container UIDs 0-65535 to host UIDs 100000-165535. This is how rootless podman works.

Capability scoping — capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, etc.) are namespace-scoped. CAP_SYS_ADMIN in a user namespace lets you mount filesystems within that namespace's mount namespace, but not on the host.
setgroups deny requirement — before Linux 3.19, unprivileged users could use setgroups() to drop to a group they weren't in after entering user namespace. 'deny' prevents this.
Cross-domain: Rootless Docker/Podman — these tools use user namespaces + subuid/subgid delegation from /etc/subuid. Understanding the mapping helps debug permission issues.

Container escape via user namespace — some exploits (CVE-2022-0847 Dirty Pipe) work because user namespaces give unprivileged users access to normally privileged code paths. This is why some systems disable user namespaces.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: User namespace (CLONE_NEWUSER) is created allowing an unprivileged user to appear as root (UID 0) inside the container
UID/GID mapping is written to /proc/<pid>/uid_map and /proc/<pid>/gid_map before the container process calls exec
'deny' is written to /proc/<pid>/setgroups before writing gid_map (required by kernel for unprivileged user namespace creation)
Container process running as mapped UID 0 can perform mount operations and other privileged operations within its namespaces
Outside the container, the process is observed running as the original unprivileged UID
All previous milestones (PID, mount, network, cgroups) function correctly when combined with user namespace

diagrams.title: Container Runtime Architecture: Satellite View
diagrams.description: System-wide map showing all five isolation layers (PID, UTS, Mount, Network, User namespaces + cgroups) surrounding a containerized process. Each layer connects to its milestone anchor. Shows the relationship between host kernel, namespace boundaries, and container process hierarchy.

diagrams.title: clone() Stack Layout for PID Namespace
diagrams.description: Memory diagram showing child stack allocation for clone(). Stack grows downward on x86-64, so the top of the stack (highest address) must be passed. Shows 16-byte alignment requirement and flags (CLONE_NEWPID | CLONE_NEWUTS | SIGCHLD).

diagrams.title: PID Namespace View Translation
diagrams.description: Data flow showing how kernel translates PIDs between namespace views. Host sees PID 12345, namespace sees PID 1. /proc/self/status NSpid field shows both. Illustrates nsproxy struct in kernel task_struct.

diagrams.title: PID 1 Zombie Reaping State Machine
diagrams.description: State evolution diagram showing child process exit → zombie state → waitpid() reaping. Shows what happens when PID 1 doesn't reap: zombies accumulate and cannot be killed. Includes code snippet of WNOHANG loop.

diagrams.title: UTS Namespace Struct Layout
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing struct uts_namespace fields: nodename, domainname. Shows how sethostname() only affects current namespace's copy. Compares host's utsname vs container's utsname.

diagrams.title: /proc/self/status NSpid Field Analysis
diagrams.description: Before/after comparison of reading /proc/self/status from inside vs outside namespace. Highlights NSpid: line showing [host-pid, namespace-pid] format.

diagrams.title: Mount Propagation Types: Shared vs Private
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of MS_SHARED (default) vs MS_PRIVATE propagation. Shows how mount event in child namespace leaks to host with shared, but is isolated with private. Includes kernel mount table state.

diagrams.title: pivot_root() Atomic Swap Sequence
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace showing: 1) bind-mount newroot to self, 2) pivot_root(newroot, oldroot_dir), 3) cd into oldroot_dir, 4) umount2 with MNT_DETACH. Shows mount table state after each step.

diagrams.title: Why chroot is Not Container Isolation
diagrams.description: Trace example of classic chroot escape via mkdir + chroot + chdir(..) + chroot. Shows why pivot_root is required for real isolation—pivot_root changes kernel mount point, not just process's root pointer.

diagrams.title: Minimal Container rootfs Directory Structure
diagrams.description: Filesystem tree showing required directories: /proc, /sys, /dev, /bin, /lib, /etc. Shows which are mount points (proc, sys, devtmpfs) vs directories. Indicates bind-mount targets for minimal /dev (null, zero, urandom).

diagrams.title: pivot_root() Error Cases: EINVAL and EBUSY
diagrams.description: Troubleshooting flowchart showing common pivot_root failures: newroot not a mount point (EINVAL), oldroot busy (EBUSY), bind-mount-to-self missing. Includes strace output examples.

diagrams.title: veth Pair: Virtual Cable Mechanics
diagrams.description: Data walk showing packet flow through veth pair. Traffic into veth0 emerges from veth1. Shows netlink message for creating pair, then moving one end to target netns via netlink RTM_NEWLINK + IFLA_NET_NS_PID.

diagrams.title: Container Network Bridge Topology
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing host with bridge ctr0 (172.17.0.1), multiple containers each with veth endpoint (172.17.0.2, 172.17.0.3), and host's eth0 connected to external network. Shows packet flow for container-to-container and container-to-internet.

diagrams.title: NAT MASQUERADE: Container to Internet
diagrams.description: Packet trace showing container (172.17.0.2) sending to 8.8.8.8. POSTROUTING MASQUERADE rule rewrites source IP to host's eth0 IP. Response packet reverse-NATed back to container IP. Shows iptables chain traversal.

diagrams.title: Network Namespace Lifecycle: Creation to Cleanup
diagrams.description: State evolution showing: unshare(CLONE_NEWNET) → lo down → veth creation → veth move to namespace → IP assign → lo up → container exec → cleanup (veth delete, bridge cleanup).

diagrams.title: Container DNS Resolution Path
diagrams.description: Data flow showing getaddrinfo() in container → /etc/resolv.conf read → DNS query to nameserver (host's bridge IP or external). Shows bind-mount vs generated resolv.conf approaches.

diagrams.title: cgroups v1 vs v2 Hierarchy Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side filesystem tree showing v1 (multiple hierarchies: /sys/fs/cgroup/memory, /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu) vs v2 (unified: /sys/fs/cgroup with subtree_control). Shows controller enablement flow.

diagrams.title: cgroup OOM Killer Decision Flow
diagrams.description: Trace showing memory usage approaching memory.max → kernel reclaim attempt → OOM triggered → cgroup process killed → oom_kill counter incremented. Compares to system-wide OOM behavior.

diagrams.title: CFS Bandwidth Controller: CPU Throttling
diagrams.description: Timeline showing 100ms period with 50ms quota. Container runs for 50ms, then throttled for 50ms. Shows cpu.stat throttled_time counter. Compares to cpu.shares (weight) vs cpu.max (hard limit).

diagrams.title: pids.max: Fork Bomb Containment
diagrams.description: Before/after showing fork bomb without pids.max (host becomes unresponsive) vs with pids.max=100 (bomb contained to 100 processes). Shows EAGAIN returned when limit hit.

diagrams.title: cgroup Cleanup Order: Why rmdir Fails
diagrams.description: State diagram showing correct cleanup order: processes exit → cgroup.procs empty → rmdir succeeds. Shows failure case: process still running → rmdir returns EBUSY. Includes cleanup on crash handling.

diagrams.title: cgroup v2 File Layout for Container
diagrams.description: Directory tree showing /sys/fs/cgroup/container-runtime/container-id/ with files: cgroup.procs, cgroup.subtree_control, memory.max, memory.current, cpu.max, cpu.stat, pids.max. Shows read vs write semantics for each.

diagrams.title: uid_map/gid_map Write Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing parent process: clone(CLONE_NEWUSER) → write 'deny' to /proc/child/setgroups → write '0 1000 1' to /proc/child/uid_map → write '0 1000 1' to /proc/child/gid_map → child execs. Shows kernel enforcement of write-once.

diagrams.title: UID Translation: Inside vs Outside Namespace
diagrams.description: Data flow showing syscall returning UID to container process. Kernel translates: container sees UID 0, host filesystem sees UID 1000. Shows /proc/pid/uid_map lookup in kernel.

diagrams.title: Capability Scoping in User Namespaces
diagrams.description: Venn diagram showing capabilities valid only within namespace (CAP_SYS_ADMIN for mounts) vs capabilities affecting host (none for unprivileged user ns). Shows capability drop on exec for setuid binaries.

diagrams.title: setgroups='deny' Security Requirement
diagrams.description: Attack trace showing pre-3.19 vulnerability: unprivileged user → user namespace → setgroups to any GID → access files via group permission. Shows how 'deny' prevents setgroups() call from succeeding.

diagrams.title: Rootless Container Limitations
diagrams.description: Comparison table showing what works in rootless (mount namespace, pivot_root, PID namespace) vs what requires host root or workarounds (veth + bridge → slirp4netns, cgroup delegation → systemd).

diagrams.title: Full Stack Integration: All Namespaces Combined
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing container process with all five namespaces active simultaneously: PID (process tree), UTS (hostname), Mount (filesystem), Network (network stack), User (UID mapping). Shows cgroup wrapper around all.

diagrams.title: Container Escape Vulnerability Taxonomy
diagrams.description: Mind map showing escape vectors: kernel exploits from user namespace (Dirty Pipe, CVE-2022-0847), misconfigured capabilities, /proc leaks, symlink attacks. Maps each to mitigation in namespace/cgroup configuration.
